title: Getting Started
text: Your entry into World of Sea Battle
content:
Beginner Information


You’ve just started World of Sea Battle and you’re wondering what to do. The first and most important thing is not to level up too quickly—at least in the beginning.


However, a very important note before we continue:

DO NOT sell your Battle Marks, Voodoo Skulls, or Escudos.

These may give you some quick gold in the beginning, but later in the game you will need these resources in the hundreds or even thousands.


The first thing you should do is level up as fast as you can to level 9 so you can get your personal island. However, from that point on, take leveling very slowly until you have your first island completely built. The reason for this is that when you reach level 11, the cost of building on your personal island increases, and at level 21 it increases even more. Before you know it, what should have cost 50 plates will cost 500 plates. This will save you a lot of resources in the long term.


Now, why do you need a personal island? It is the most important thing in the game. You get reduced costs for producing items like plates, you can send missions from your island to collect resources from mines (saving you from manually emptying them), and you gain the ability to fast travel across the map to your personal island.


Where should I build my first personal island?

This is entirely up to you; however, I recommend building it at St Jean (south of Fiji).

As this is one of the main trade hubs in the world, there are two locations here where you can build your first island. This comes down to personal preference.


The first location


This is a great location, as you will always have wind access to the port of St Jean.


The second location (my personal favourite)


This is a little closer to Los Catuano, which might be more useful if you are splitting your production between multiple ports (more on this later).


Upon purchasing your personal island, the most important thing to do is buy the workstation and make sure you select plates, as plates will be what you need to build up your island—unless you have friends who can give you 190 plates to fully build it before you reach level 11.


The second most important thing to build is missions. This will allow you to send out boats to collect supplies produced at your mines so you don’t have to.


To fast travel to your island, you will need a lighthouse. This isn’t required, but it’s very useful for getting from one side of the map to the other quickly.


Manpower

Manpower is used by your personal island to produce goods and to send out missions. You will get 1 manpower every hour, or 2 if you build the pub. You can also increase your manpower by dropping off captives at your island. Captives can be obtained early game from ferrymen (trade ships) in exchange for fish, and later by boarding ships once you have the right perks and the right ship.


Missions

To send out a mission, simply click on your personal island on the world map. This will send out ships to collect goods from your mines. These ships appear blue on the map, cannot be seen directly in-game, and cannot be destroyed. Each mission requires manpower and costs 50 manpower.

Mine location also plays a big role, as missions have a maximum range of 7000 units. This is quite far, but it won’t cover the entire map. To check distance, hold down CTRL on the world map to draw a line between your island and a mine—you’ll see the distance and whether it’s in range or not. You can increase the range of your missions by purchasing the observatory on your personal island, which increases the range to 8000 units. This costs 500,000 gold, so it is something for later in the game once you are established.


Mines

When you’re just starting out, you can have a maximum of 2 mines. The first two you should focus on are wood and iron so you can produce planks. Your closest iron mines are northwest of Fiji at a place called Freedom Bay, or east of Bord Radel.


For wood, you also have two options: the mine between Fiji and St Jean, or the wood mine west of Radel.


Once you have leveled up enough and unlocked additional mines (work force perk & , you should focus on coal mines and possibly a farm, however the farm depends on whether you want to spend money on buying the fabric or not to make canvas.


I recommend upgrading these mines to level 2 as quickly as possible, but not beyond that as you will likely move these mines into the pvp area in the center of the map as mines inside this area give bonus production however you cant access this area untill your level 12 so keep that in mind.


Production

It is always cheaper to make things yourself, so you will need to get established. Until you have enough funds, you are going to be limited to only one production per port. To get a second production in a port, you need to upgrade the warehouse to level 5, which costs a lot of gold and isn’t really worth it unless you can obtain a license. A license typically costs between 250k–320k; if it’s more than this, it’s not worth buying.


I would advise your initial setup to be:

St Jean as beams

Radel as bulkheads

Los Catuano as canvas


Radel will only be temporary until you relocate or upgrade St Jean for double production. The reason for Radel is that it does not have shallow waters, making it easier for your larger ships to transport heavy goods like bulkheads.


Los Catuano is the closest lighthouse-to-lighthouse port from St Jean. You will either transport fabric there or buy it from the market whilst there and produce it in this port.


St Jean is a great place for production and is the center of trade and commerce, meaning items will almost always sell or be available to buy quickly.


Ideally long term you will be wanting to do any mass producing on your personal island as the reduction is 20%, so if your planning on crafting 100 bulkheads for example, you would switch your production on your personal island which costs manpower to do so and in doing so you will save yourself 1000 iron and 400 resin per 100 bulkheads you make, however in the begining it may just be easier for you to just to produce them in Radel and save on the time of switching and farming captives for some loss in resources, however this all comes down to how you want to play the game.



Making Gold

There are lots of ways to make gold:

Boarding enemy ships (with the right perks)

Skirmishes (PvP zones with gold rewards)

Quests (rumours from taverns)

Trade routes

Chest hunting in PvP areas

Endless Sea farming

Building and selling ships



Boarding enemy ships

if you have the right perks and the right ship you can decimate you enemies and board them for 10% of their gold in pvp areas


Skirmishes

red circles that appear on the map every hour if your close swing by but be carefull it is a pvp area and not allways friendly, all you have to do is sit inside the inner red ring and you will get x amount of gold every second depending on how many ships are there, more ships = less gold per second, if you sink a boat inside this area you will get 10k gold for each sinking


Quests

also known as rumours from the tavern at ports, you can get rumours at all the ports around the world and offer various tasks from dropping off goods to sinking ships.


Trade routes

I personally think this is the best way to make money, you take market goods from one side of the map to the other this link here will take you to a trade map, clicking on each port on this map will show you what to buy and what to sell to make profit, the only downside to this is you will need a good sized ship like the Russia and a decent amount of gold to get you started off doing trade runs


Chest hunting

can give you really good loot to sell but it requires you to be level 12 and comes at very high risks alot of people hunt these chests as they are in the PVP area and its not as simple as sailing in and sailing out you will need a fast ship and good cannons to be able to shoot and run


Endless sea

not many people farm in the endless sea as it is a pvp area but you can get some decent loot from here the only problem is the terrible winds which may capsize your ship if your not carefull and however most of the NPC ships here will give you Escudos for sinking them


Building and selling ships

Making ships and selling them, making ships and selling them can be very profitable but also end up costing you money by never selling, you need to know the costs to make a ship and if there is a demand for it to try and make a profit, for me the most profitable ship at the moment is the Russia, alot of new players are joining and needing trade ships and dont quiet have the resources or know how to make their ships but have gold to buy a ship, so what costs me 75-78k in raw resources (if i dont already have it my warehouse from my mines) i can turn into 150k+ sale giving me 50-80k ish profit give or take for each Russia i build depending on how cheap or how high i decide to list it, when listing ships make sure there isnt already alot of these ships already on the market, for example if there is 20 Russias for sale, you wouldnt go and make a Russia and try and sell it as everyone will be competing to drive the price down so you end up making less money, think smart and you will find the right ship to sell at the right time and place for good profit



Endless Sea - PvP Area - Decoy/Black Flag

It is very important to make sure you are not carrying too much gold when doing these activities. If you get boarded, you can lose up to 10% of your total gold. before doing these activities make sure to store your gold in chests or buy as many trade goods as possible and try to keep your gold in tradegoods inside your warehouses.

title: Faction Rep
text: how to earn faction rep and requirements for guilds
content:
Factions


to join a military faction, you need to have rep with the faction you are trying to join, the minimum required amount of rep to join a militant faction is 33 and if you drop below 33 the game will auto kick you from the guild after a period of time, so its good to keep your rep above 50 just to be safe, you can loose rep by sinking players of trade unions (unless your pirates)


the easiest and quickest way to obtain rep for a faction is to run all the neutral ports and do transport rumours from the tavern, you can pickup 3 quests at a time and complete them all and they are pretty quick to do


the best location to do this on the map is over by St Jean and a good route would be

St Jean -> Puerto Salada -> Santa Marta -> Brandport -> Freedom bay


each transport mission will give you 1 rep in the faction of your choice and you can do 3 transports at each of these locations so that would be 15 rep per day just in transports, however you can also do the other rumours from the tavern like privateering and assistance which if you complete all of these at each of the ports and it would give you 45 rep for completing all 3 types of quests, however it is also worth noting that you can use a skill point and 10,000 gold to increase the amount of quests you can pickup to a maximum of 4 quests at one time, the skill point is under the crafting tab and it called combat log




however you can also complete tavern quests inside a port owned by the faction you are trying to join which will give you rep in that faction, for example if Bord Radel is currently owned by the Kai and Severia faction any quest you complete here will give you rep in Kai and Severia, however if it was owned by Antilia it would give you rep into Antilia



Before starting quests, you can try to optimize your trade route if you have scrolls, you can obtain these from sinking NPS ships, rescuing people off of boats, dig icons, floatsum and completing quests however the most reliable way i have found to get them is via rescuing people of the boats as it rewards anywhere from 1-6 scrolls per rescue this is completely R&G so may the gods be with you. after you have some scrolls re-roll the quests by clicking refresh this will cost 1 scroll each time.




the idea here is to get 2-3 of the quests to be in exactly the same location, you can see the location by hovering over each of the 3 icons




a perfect roll would be where all 3 quests deliver to the same location, so if i was re-rolling at St Jean and i got all 3 quests to deliver to Fuji that would be a perfect roll as i only need to do 1 trip to Fiji rather than 1 trip to Fiji, 1 trip to Bord Radel and then 1 trip to Freedom bay anyway you get the idea, try and roll if you can for 2-3 being the same location to save you time doing rep runs



After accepting a quest from the tavern you will see markers appear on the map around the port these can change slightly depending on the quest however they are not hard to miss, sail over to the location if your doing it at a neutral port you will see reputation +1 (faction of choice)



after sailing over and interacting with the merchant click on choose a faction



after clicking choose faction you will be presented with a list of factions to choose from choose the faction which applies to you



after selecting your faction, accept the quest and complete it, upon completion you will receive rep in the faction that you picked.

title: Ship Damage
text: How damage is calculated during combat
content:
Ship Damage


alot of people have been confused around the concept of ship damage and how this works, a very simple explanation is, shoot them in the rear or with direct broadsides, however if your looking for more of a break down i will explain the damage calculations further on.




The game breaks down the damage calculation as follows


Damage = ((Penetration - armour * angle) * part modifier) * damage modifier


Penetration:

this is the penetration damage of your cannons, the higher the penetration rating the better, as the main base calculation is penetration - armour so if the armour is 10 and the penetration is 4 its a total of 6 base points which then gets multiplied by the angle at which your shooting the boat at.


Angle:

angle plays a huge role in damage, cannon balls fired from your ship hit the target ship and go through the ship, so if you hit the ship at an angle the cannon ball wont go through the ship but graze along side it doing alot less damage and can fluctuate the damage on a shot quiet alot if you base shot is 17 your angle shoot could fluctuate anywhere from 5-15 depending on the angle the game values look to be calculating this based on 1.0 - 5.0 based on the angle


Armor:

this is the broadside armour rating of the ship for example a black wind has 4 broadside armour


Part Modifier:

this is the part of the ship that is being hit, if your hitting the stern its 1.37 modifier but if your hitting the bow your getting a 0.9 modifier, if your hitting broadsides its 1, this means its better to hit the ship in the rear to deal more damage.


Damage modifier:

this applies to any buffs which you put on for example a red flare boosts damage by 7%



so now if we start to apply these numbers and values to 2 ships, lets say its black wind vs black wind


black wind (a): has 16-pdr carronades at 21.5 penetration


black wind (b): has no buffs, no upgrades


if black wind (a) and black wind (b) were side by side and black wind (a) shot black wind (b) with a full broad side and hit every shot the damage would be calculated as following


Damage = (21.5 - 4 * 1) * 1) * 1


Damage = 17.5 per shot


the game doesn't apply damage in decimals and rounds to the nearest whole number so if its .4 it would round down if its .5, .6 it will round up so for this calculation it will be 18 damage per shot and as there 16 cannons per side that is 288 damage for a full perfect broadside not accounting for angle



now there is a caveat to this calculation in how stern and bow hits are calculated, bow and stern hits do not account for angle, so regardless of which angle your shooting at the full damage is applied


the calculation for a stern hit (rear of the boat) is as follows:


Damage = (21.5 - 4 * 1) * 1.37) * 1


which would equate to 23.975 and with the rounding it would equate to 24 damage hit on the stern (rear of the boat)



the calculation for a bow is as follows


Damage = (21.5 - 4 * 1) * 0.9) * 1



which would equate to 15.75 and with rounding it would be 16 damage per shot



now if you are looking to calculate the angle of a shot, we can break it down into a simple equation:


angle multiplier = 1 + k × sin(theta)


theta = the angle of the target

k = 2.4 (based on testing)

Perpendicular shot (0 degrees)


If the ships are perfectly side-on:


angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × sin(0)

angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × 0

angle multiplier would equal 1


30 degrees shot


angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × sin(30)

angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × 0.5

angle multiplier would equal 2.2


90 degrees shot


angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × sin(90)

angle multiplier = 1 + 2.4 × 1

angle multiplier would equal 3.4




so using the examples above as before black wind (a) vs black wind (b)


if black wind (a) was 45 degrees shooting onto black wind (b)


angle multiplier would be as follows

sin(45) = 0.707


angle = 1 + (2.4 * 0.7)

angle = 2.68


we then plug this into our damage formula which for reference again is

Damage = ((Penetration - armour * angle) * part modifier) * damage modifier


damage = (21.5 - 4 * 2.68) * 1 * 1


damage = 10.78 rounded up to 11


so 11 per cannon ball hit at an angle of 45 degrees using 21.5 penetration cannons




now there is a a few more factors to take into account which i have not covered and will update this shortly with is different shot types(heated shot, heavy shot, saxon shot etc..), each shot types affect penetration which in turn boosts/nerfs the damage done, i will also cover in the future tick damage for fire and how this is all calculated however for now this should be a good basis to work from
